g.data-narration: Welcome to Exhaustive Search - the fundamental technique that systematically explores all possible solutions to find the optimal answer.
g.text: Analysis and Design of Algorithms - university 2022 Scheme

g.data-narration: Exhaustive search examines every possible candidate systematically, guaranteeing the optimal solution but at computational cost.
g.text: A fundamental algorithmic technique that systematically examines ALL candidates
for the solution and checks each against problem constraints.

g.data-narration: This approach guarantees optimality but requires checking every possibility, making it suitable for small problem sizes.
g.text: ✗ Can Be Slow for Large n

g.data-narration: Linear search examines each element sequentially until the target is found or the list is exhausted, with O(n) time complexity.
g.text: Examines elements one by one until target is found

g.data-narration: String matching slides the pattern across the text, comparing each position until a complete match or failure occurs.
Depth-First Search explores deeply along each branch using a stack before backtracking, ideal for path finding and cycle detection.
g.text: Explores as deep as possible before backtracking • Uses Stack (LIFO)

g.data-narration: Breadth-First Search explores level by level using a queue, guaranteeing the shortest path in unweighted graphs.
g.text: Explores all nodes at current depth before moving deeper • Uses Queue (FIFO)

g.data-narration: DFS uses stack memory and finds any path, while BFS uses queue and guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs.
g.text: When to use DFS: Maze solving, topological sort, cycle detection
When to use BFS: Shortest path, level-order traversal, closest nodes

g.data-narration: Backtracking builds solutions incrementally and abandons paths that cannot lead to valid solutions, as shown in N-Queens.
g.text: Builds solutions incrementally, undoes choices that lead to dead ends
for row in 0..n-1: if safe: place; recurse; backtrack
Key: Try → Test → Undo if failed (backtrack)

g.data-narration: Branch and Bound uses bounds on the objective function to prune branches that cannot yield better solutions than current best.
g.text: Uses upper/lower bounds to prune search tree branches

g.data-narration: Generating all permutations uses recursive swapping, exploring all n! arrangements systematically with backtracking.
g.text: Recursive approach: swap, recurse, backtrack (swap back)
else: for i = l to r: swap(A[l],A[i]); permute(l+1,r); swap back

g.data-narration: Finding maximum in an array uses linear search, tracking the largest value seen so far with O(n) time complexity.
The 0/1 knapsack problem evaluates all 2^n subsets to find maximum value within weight capacity, achieving optimal solution.
